a new language, identify knowledge gaps, adapt to their learning style, and adjust its teaching methods over
months or even years. Furthermore, a science agent could pursue ambitious goals, such as discovering a new
material or reducing carbon dioxide. Such an agent could analyse real-world observations over an extended
period, developing and running simulations, and suggesting real-world experiments or interventions.

period, developing and running simulations, and suggesting real-world experiments or interventions.
In each case, the agent takes a sequence of steps so as to maximise long-term success with respect to the
specified goal. An individual step may not provide any immediate benefit, or may even be detrimental in the
short term, but may nevertheless contribute in aggregate to longer term success. This contrasts strongly with
current AI systems that provide immediate responses to requests, without any ability to measure or optimise
the future consequences of their actions on the environment.
Actions and Observations
Agents in the era of experience will act autonomously in the real world. LLMs in the era of human data

Actions and Observations
Agents in the era of experience will act autonomously in the real world. LLMs in the era of human data
focused primarily on human-privileged actions and observations that output text to a user, and input text from
the user back into the agent. This differs markedly from natural intelligence, in which an animal interacts
with its environment through motor control and sensors. While animals, and most notably humans, may
communicate with other animals, this occurs through the same interface as other sensorimotor control rather
than a privileged channel.
It has long been recognised that LLMs may also invoke actions in the digital world, for example by
calling APIs (see for example, [43]). Initially, these capabilities came largely from human examples of

calling APIs (see for example, [43]). Initially, these capabilities came largely from human examples of
tool-use, rather than from the experience of the agent. However, coding and tool-use capabilities have built
increasingly upon execution feedback [17, 7, 12], where the agent actually runs code and observes what
happens. Recently, a new wave of prototype agents have started to interact with computers in an even more
general manner, by using the same interface that humans use to operate a computer [3, 15, 24]. These
changes herald a transition from exclusively human-privileged communication, to much more autonomous
interactions where the agent is able to act independently in the world. Such agents will be able to actively

interactions where the agent is able to act independently in the world. Such agents will be able to actively
explore the world, adapt to changing environments, and discover strategies that might never occur to a human.
These richer interactions will provide a means to autonomously understand and control the digital world.
The agent may use ‘human-friendly’ actions and observations such as user interfaces, that naturally facilitate
communication and collaboration with the user. The agent may also take ‘machine-friendly’ actions that
execute code and call APIs, allowing the agent to act autonomously in service of its goals. In the era of
experience, agents will also interact with the real world via digital interfaces. For example, a scientific agent

experience, agents will also interact with the real world via digital interfaces. For example, a scientific agent
could monitor environmental sensors, remotely operate a telescope, or control a robotic arm in a laboratory
to autonomously conduct experiments.
Rewards
What if experiential agents could learn from external events and signals, and not just human preferences?
Human-centric LLMs typically optimise for rewards based on human prejudgement: an expert observes
the agent’s action and decides whether it is a good action, or picks the best agent action among multiple al-
ternatives. For example, an expert may judge a health agent’s advice, an educational assistant’s teaching, or a

ternatives. For example, an expert may judge a health agent’s advice, an educational assistant’s teaching, or a
scientist agent’s suggested experiment. The fact that these rewards or preferences are determined by humans
in absence of their consequences, rather than measuring the effect of those actions on the environment, means
that they are not directly grounded in the reality of the world. Relying on human prejudgement in this manner
usually leads to an impenetrable ceiling on the agent’s performance: the agent cannot discover better strate-
gies that are underappreciated by the human rater. To discover new ideas that go far beyond existing human
knowledge, it is instead necessary to use grounded rewards: signals that arise from the environment itself.

knowledge, it is instead necessary to use grounded rewards: signals that arise from the environment itself.
For example, a health assistant could ground the user’s health goals into a reward based on a combination of
3
signals such as their resting heart rate, sleep duration, and activity levels, while an educational assistant could
use exam results to provide a grounded reward for language learning. Similarly, a science agent with a goal
to reduce global warming might use a reward based on empirical observations of carbon dioxide levels, while
a goal to discover a stronger material might be grounded in a combination of measurements from a materials
simulator, such as tensile strength or Young’s modulus.

simulator, such as tensile strength or Young’s modulus.
Grounded rewards may arise from humans that are part of the agent’s environment. 2 For example, a
human user could report whether they found a cake tasty, how fatigued they are after exercising, or the level
of pain from a headache, enabling an assistant agent to provide better recipes, refine its fitness suggestions,
or improve its recommended medication. Such rewards measure the consequence of the agent’s actions
within their environment, and should ultimately lead to better assistance than a human expert that prejudges
a proposed cake recipe, exercise program, or treatment program.
Where do rewards come from, if not from human data? Once agents become connected to the world

Where do rewards come from, if not from human data? Once agents become connected to the world
through rich action and observation spaces (see above), there will be no shortage of grounded signals to
provide a basis for reward. In fact, the world abounds with quantities such as cost, error rates, hunger, pro-
ductivity, health metrics, climate metrics, profit, sales, exam results, success, visits, yields, stocks, likes,
income, pleasure/pain, economic indicators, accuracy, power, distance, speed, efficiency, or energy consump-
tion. In addition there are innumerable additional signals arising from the occurrence of specific events, or
from features derived from raw sequences of observations and actions.

from features derived from raw sequences of observations and actions.
One could in principle create a variety of distinct agents, each optimising for one grounded signal as its
reward. There is an argument that even a single such reward signal, optimised with great effectiveness, may
be sufficient to induce broadly capable intelligence [34]. 3 This is because the achievement of a simple goal
in a complex environment may often require a wide variety of skills to be mastered.
However, the pursuit of a single reward signal does not on the surface appear to meet the requirements of a
general-purpose AI that can be steered reliably towards arbitrary user-desired behaviours. Is the autonomous

general-purpose AI that can be steered reliably towards arbitrary user-desired behaviours. Is the autonomous
optimisation of grounded, non-human reward signals therefore in opposition to the requirements of modern
AI systems? We argue that this is not necessarily the case, by sketching one approach that may meet these
desiderata; other approaches may also be possible.
The idea is to flexibly adapt the reward, based on grounded signals, in a user-guided manner. For example,
the reward function could be defined by a neural network that takes the agent’s interactions with both the user
and the environment as input, and outputs a scalar reward. This allows the reward to select or combine
together signals from the environment in a manner that depends upon the user’s goal. For example, a user

together signals from the environment in a manner that depends upon the user’s goal. For example, a user
might specify a broad goal such as ’improve my fitness’ and the reward function might return a function
of the user’s heart rate, sleep duration, and steps taken. Or the user might specify a goal of ‘help me learn
Spanish’ and the reward function could return the user’s Spanish exam results.
Furthermore, users could provide feedback during the learning process, such as their satisfaction level,
which could be used to fine-tune the reward function. The reward function can then adapt over time, to
improve the way in which it selects or combines signals, and to identify and correct any misalignment. This

improve the way in which it selects or combines signals, and to identify and correct any misalignment. This
can also be understood as a bi-level optimisation process that optimises user feedback as the top-level goal,
and optimises grounded signals from the environment at the low level.4 In this way, a small amount of human
data may facilitate a large amount of autonomous learning.

Planning and Reasoning
Will the era of experience change the way that agents plan and reason? Recently, there has been significant
progress using LLMs that can reason, or “think” with language [23, 14, 10], by following a chain of thought
before outputting a response [16]. Conceptually, LLMs can act as a universal computer [30]: an LLM can

before outputting a response [16]. Conceptually, LLMs can act as a universal computer [30]: an LLM can
append tokens into its own context, allowing it to execute arbitrary algorithms before outputting a final result.
In the era of human data, these reasoning methods have been explicitly designed to imitate human thought
processes. For example, LLMs have been prompted to emit human-like chains of thought [16], imitate traces
of human thinking [42], or to reinforce steps of thinking that match human examples [18]. The reasoning
process may be fine-tuned further to produce thinking traces that match the correct answer, as determined by
human experts [44].
However, it is highly unlikely that human language provides the optimal instance of a universal computer.

human experts [44].
However, it is highly unlikely that human language provides the optimal instance of a universal computer.
More efficient mechanisms of thought surely exist, using non-human languages that may for example utilise
symbolic, distributed, continuous, or differentiable computations. A self-learning system can in principle
discover or improve such approaches by learning how to think from experience. For example, AlphaProof
learned to formally prove complex theorems in a manner quite different to human mathematicians [20].
Furthermore, the principle of a universal computer only addresses the internal computation of the agent;
it does not connect it to the realities of the external world. An agent trained to imitate human thoughts or

it does not connect it to the realities of the external world. An agent trained to imitate human thoughts or
even to match human expert answers may inherit fallacious methods of thought deeply embedded within that
data, such as flawed assumptions or inherent biases. For example, if an agent had been trained to reason using
human thoughts and expert answers from 5,000 years ago it may have reasoned about a physical problem in
terms of animism; 1,000 years ago it may have reasoned in theistic terms; 300 years ago it may have reasoned
in terms of Newtonian mechanics; and 50 years ago in terms of quantum mechanics. Progressing beyond
each method of thought required interaction with the real world: making hypotheses, running experiments,

each method of thought required interaction with the real world: making hypotheses, running experiments,
observing results, and updating principles accordingly. Similarly, an agent must be grounded in real-world
data in order to overturn fallacious methods of thought. This grounding provides a feedback loop, allowing
the agent to test its inherited assumptions against reality and discover new principles that are not limited by
current, dominant modes of human thought. Without this grounding, an agent, no matter how sophisticated,
will become an echo chamber of existing human knowledge. To move beyond this, agents must actively
engage with the world, collect observational data, and use that data to iteratively refine their understanding,

engage with the world, collect observational data, and use that data to iteratively refine their understanding,
mirroring in many ways the process that has driven human scientific progress.
One possible way to directly ground thinking in the external world is to build a world model [37] that
predicts the consequences of the agent’s actions upon the world, including predicting reward. For example,
a health assistant might consider making a recommendation for a local gym or a health podcast. The agent’s
world model might predict how a user’s heart rate or sleep patterns might subsequently change following this
action, as well as predicting future dialogue with the user. This allows the agent to plan [36, 29] directly in

action, as well as predicting future dialogue with the user. This allows the agent to plan [36, 29] directly in
terms of its own actions and their causal effect upon the world. As the agent continues to interact with the
world throughout its stream of experience, its dynamics model is continually updated to correct any errors
in its predictions. Given a world model, an agent may apply scalable planning methods that improve the
predicted performance of the agent.
Planning and reasoning methods are not mutually exclusive: an agent may apply internal LLM computa-
tions to select each action during planning, or to simulate and evaluate the consequences of those actions.
Why Now?
Learning from experience is not new. Reinforcement learning systems have previously mastered a large

Why Now?
Learning from experience is not new. Reinforcement learning systems have previously mastered a large
number of complex tasks that were represented in a simulator with a clear reward signal (c.f., approximately,
the “era of simulation” in Figure 1). For example, RL methods equalled or exceeded human performance

through self-play in board games such as backgammon [39], Go [31], chess [32], poker [22, 6] and Strat-
ego [26]; video games such as Atari [21], StarCraft II [40], Dota 2 [4] and Gran Turismo [41]; dextrous
manipulation tasks such as Rubik’s cube [1]; and resource management tasks such as data center cooling

manipulation tasks such as Rubik’s cube [1]; and resource management tasks such as data center cooling
[13]. Furthermore, powerful RL agents such as AlphaZero [33] exhibited impressive and potentially unlim-
ited scalability with the size of the neural network, the quantity of interactive experience, and the duration of
thinking time. However, agents based on this paradigm did not leap the gap between simulation (closed prob-
lems with singular, precisely defined rewards) to reality (open-ended problems with a plurality of seemingly
ill-defined rewards).
The era of human data offered an appealing solution. Massive corpuses of human data contain examples
of natural language for a huge diversity of tasks. Agents trained on this data achieved a wide range of com-

of natural language for a huge diversity of tasks. Agents trained on this data achieved a wide range of com-
petencies compared to the more narrow successes of the era of simulation. Consequently, the methodology
of experiential RL was largely discarded in favour of more general-purpose agents, resulting in a widespread
transition to human-centric AI.
However, something was lost in this transition: an agent’s ability to self-discover its own knowledge.
For example, AlphaZero discovered fundamentally new strategies for chess and Go, changing the way that
humans play these games [28, 45]. The era of experience will reconcile this ability with the level of task-
generality achieved in the era of human data. This will become possible, as outlined above, when agents are

generality achieved in the era of human data. This will become possible, as outlined above, when agents are
able to autonomously act and observe in streams of real-world experience [11], and where the rewards may
be flexibly connected to any of an abundance of grounded, real-world signals. The advent of autonomous
agents that interact with complex, real-world action spaces [3, 15, 24], alongside powerful RL methods that
can solve open-ended problems in rich reasoning spaces [20, 10] suggests that the transition to the era of
experience is imminent.

Reinforcement Learning Methods
Reinforcement learning (RL) has a rich history that is deeply rooted in autonomous learning, where agents

6
Reinforcement Learning Methods
Reinforcement learning (RL) has a rich history that is deeply rooted in autonomous learning, where agents
learn for themselves through direct interaction with their environment. Early RL research yielded a suite of
powerful concepts and algorithms. For example, temporal difference learning [35] enabled agents to estimate
future rewards, leading to breakthroughs such as superhuman performance in backgammon [39]. Exploration
techniques, driven by optimism or curiosity, were developed to help agents discover creative new behaviors
and avoid getting stuck in suboptimal routines [2]. Methods like the Dyna algorithm enabled agents to
build and learn from models of their world, allowing them to plan and reason about future actions [36,

build and learn from models of their world, allowing them to plan and reason about future actions [36,
29]. Concepts like options and inter/intra-option learning facilitated temporal abstraction, enabling agents to
reason over longer timescales and break down complex tasks into manageable sub-goals [38].
The rise of human-centric LLMs, however, shifted the focus away from autonomous learning and towards
leveraging human knowledge. Techniques like RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) [9,
25] and methods for aligning language models with human reasoning [44] proved incredibly effective, driving
rapid progress in AI capabilities. These approaches, while powerful, often bypassed core RL concepts: RLHF

rapid progress in AI capabilities. These approaches, while powerful, often bypassed core RL concepts: RLHF
side-stepped the need for value functions by invoking human experts in place of machine-estimated values,
strong priors from human data reduced the reliance on exploration, and reasoning in human-centric terms
lessened the need for world models and temporal abstraction.
However, it could be argued that the shift in paradigm has thrown out the baby with the bathwater. While
human-centric RL has enabled an unprecedented breadth of behaviours, it has also imposed a new ceiling
on the agent’s performance: agents cannot go beyond existing human knowledge. Furthermore, the era of
human data has focused predominantly on RL methods that are designed for short episodes of ungrounded,

human data has focused predominantly on RL methods that are designed for short episodes of ungrounded,
human interaction, and are not suitable for long streams of grounded, autonomous interaction.
The era of experience presents an opportunity to revisit and improve classic RL concepts. This era will
bring new ways to think about reward functions that are flexibly grounded in observational data. It will
revisit value functions and methods to estimate them from long streams with as yet incomplete sequences. It
will bring principled yet practical methods for real-world exploration that discover new behaviours that are
radically different from human priors. Novel approaches to world models will be developed that capture the

radically different from human priors. Novel approaches to world models will be developed that capture the
complexities of grounded interactions. New methods for temporal abstraction will allow agents to reason, in
terms of experience, over ever-longer time horizons. By building upon the foundations of RL and adapting
its core principles to the challenges of this new era, we can unlock the full potential of autonomous learning
and pave the way to truly superhuman intelligence.
Consequences
The advent of the era of experience, where AI agents learn from their interactions with the world, promises a
future profoundly different from anything we have seen before. This new paradigm, while offering immense

future profoundly different from anything we have seen before. This new paradigm, while offering immense
potential, also presents important risks and challenges that demand careful consideration, including but not
limited to the following points.
On the positive side, experiential learning will unlock unprecedented capabilities. In everyday life, per-
sonalized assistants will leverage continuous streams of experience to adapt to individuals’ health, educa-
tional, or professional needs towards long-term goals over the course of months or years. Perhaps most
transformative will be the acceleration of scientific discovery. AI agents will autonomously design and con-
duct experiments in fields like materials science, medicine, or hardware design. By continuously learning

duct experiments in fields like materials science, medicine, or hardware design. By continuously learning
from the results of their own experiments, these agents could rapidly explore new frontiers of knowledge,
leading to the development of novel materials, drugs, and technologies at an unprecedented pace.
However, this new era also presents significant and novel challenges. While the automation of human
7
capabilities promises to boost productivity, these improvements could also lead to job displacement. Agents
may even be able to exhibit capabilities previously considered the exclusive realm of humanity, such as long-
term problem-solving, innovation, and a deep understanding of real world consequences.

term problem-solving, innovation, and a deep understanding of real world consequences.
Furthermore, whilst general concerns exist around the potential misuse of any AI, heightened risks may
arise from agents that can autonomously interact with the world over extended periods of time to achieve
long-term goals. By default, this provides fewer opportunities for humans to intervene and mediate the
agent’s actions, and therefore requires a high bar of trust and responsibility. Moving away from human data
and human modes of thinking may also make future AI systems harder to interpret.
However, whilst acknowledging that experiential learning will increase certain safety risks, and that fur-

However, whilst acknowledging that experiential learning will increase certain safety risks, and that fur-
ther research is surely required to ensure a safe transition into the era of experience, we should also recognise
that it may also provide some important safety benefits.
Firstly, an experiential agent is aware of the environment it is situated within, and its behaviour can adapt
over time to changes in that environment. Any pre-programmed system, including a fixed AI system, can
be unaware of its environmental context, and become maladapted to the changing world into which it is
deployed. For example, a critical piece of hardware may malfunction, a pandemic might cause rapid societal

deployed. For example, a critical piece of hardware may malfunction, a pandemic might cause rapid societal
change, or a new scientific discovery may trigger a cascade of rapid technological developments. By contrast,
an experiential agent could observe and learn to circumvent malfunctioning hardware, adjust to rapid societal
change, or embrace and build upon new science and technology. Perhaps even more importantly, the agent
could recognise when its behaviour is triggering human concern, dissatisfaction, or distress, and adaptively
modify its behaviour to avoid these negative consequences.
Secondly, the agent’s reward function may itself be adapted through experience, for example using the bi-

Secondly, the agent’s reward function may itself be adapted through experience, for example using the bi-
level optimisation described earlier (see Rewards). Importantly, this means that misaligned reward functions
can often be incrementally corrected over time by trial and error. For example, rather than blindly optimising
a signal, such as the maximisation of paperclips [5], the reward function could be modified, based upon
indications of human concern, before paperclip production consumes all of the Earth’s resources. This is
analogous to the way that humans set goals for each other, and then adapt those goals if they observe people
gaming the system, neglecting long-term well-being, or causing undesired negative consequences; although

gaming the system, neglecting long-term well-being, or causing undesired negative consequences; although
also like human goal-setting, there is no guarantee of perfect alignment.
Finally, advancements relying on physical experience are inherently constrained by the time it takes to
execute actions in the real world and observe their consequences. For example, the development of a new
drug, even with AI-assisted design, still requires real-world trials that cannot be completed overnight. This
may provide a natural brake on the pace of potential AI self-improvement.
Conclusion
The era of experience marks a pivotal moment in the evolution of AI. Building on today’s strong foundations,
but moving beyond the limitations of human-derived data, agents will increasingly learn from their own

but moving beyond the limitations of human-derived data, agents will increasingly learn from their own
interactions with the world. Agents will autonomously interact with environments through rich observations
and actions. They will continue to adapt over the course of lifelong streams of experience. Their goals
will be directable towards any combination of grounded signals. Furthermore, agents will utilise powerful
non-human reasoning, and construct plans that are grounded in the consequences of the agent’s actions upon
its environment. Ultimately, experiential data will eclipse the scale and quality of human generated data.
This paradigm shift, accompanied by algorithmic advancements in RL, will unlock in many domains new
capabilities that surpass those possessed by any human.

Guidelines and Definitions for the Book 
 
This is a unique hybrid-genre book, blending a novella-style story about Rob Ryan's life with 
an entrepreneurial guide that includes the principles of the Sunflower Code, a business 
framework developed by Rob Ryan. 
 
The content of this book is based on the following sources: 
1. The dramatic film script I wrote and directed stored in the file titled: "Document 1 – Film 
Script: The Man Who Saved the Internet with a Sunflower." 
2. A draft book written by Michael Levin about Rob Ryan, stored in the file titled: "Document 2 
– Michael Levin’s Book Chapters 1-13." 
3. A guidebook on the Sunflower Code, stored in the file titled: "Document 3 – V4 Sunflower 
Manuscript."
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

– Michael Levin’s Book Chapters 1-13." 
3. A guidebook on the Sunflower Code, stored in the file titled: "Document 3 – V4 Sunflower 
Manuscript." 
4. The script for the film *License2Dream*, stored in the file titled: "Document 4 – Film Script: 
License2Dream." 
5. The app scripts for *License2Dream*, stored in the file titled: "Document 5 – 
License2Dream App Scripts." 
 
The goal of this book is to serve as a digital book where readers can engage with the content 
in multiple ways: 
1. **As a narrative drama/novella** 
2. **As a guidebook on the Sunflower Code** 
3. **As a combination of both (drama and guidance) ** 
 
In the full digital version of the book, there will be embedded "expansion buttons" within the

In the full digital version of the book, there will be embedded "expansion buttons" within the 
text. If a reader clicks on one of these, they will access additional content such as videos, 
audio clips, articles, and more, expanding on the topic at hand. 
 
Writing approach: 
1. **Writing the full chronological narrative** of Rob Ryan’s story. 
2. **Adding extensions** to the narrative to create a teaching tool and provide resources that 
will help entrepreneurs make better decisions and avoid mistakes. Like any entrepreneur, Rob 
Ryan made both good and bad decisions, and through his story, we hope to provide valuable 
insights to millions of entrepreneurs worldwide, especially young ones, to help them achieve 
significant success.

insights to millions of entrepreneurs worldwide, especially young ones, to help them achieve 
significant success. 
 
One of the key principles Rob Ryan emphasizes is that everyone must strive to be the "king" 
or "queen" of their chosen field. 
 
Golden Rules for Writing the Book: 
1. **Avoid unnecessary length. ** 
2. **Keep the story engaging and compelling. ** 
3. **Maintain the humor that characterizes the protagonist. ** 
4. **Focus on dialogues and minimize lectures. ** 
 
 
 
 
3 
 
The Path to Becoming the "King Of" 
Introduction to the Sunflower Code Story 
 
The ballroom was packed with Silicon Valley’s finest—visionaries, innovators, and titans of the tech 
world. The prestigious Ernst & Young awards ceremony was in full swing, and the air buzzed with
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
world. The prestigious Ernst & Young awards ceremony was in full swing, and the air buzzed with 
anticipation. Yet, at the table marked Ascend Communications, only two seats were occupied. Rob 
Ryan sat with his wife, Terry, by his side. The other chairs, meant for his team, remained 
conspicuously empty. The silence between them was telling. None of his colleagues had shown up. 
It wasn’t a mistake—someone had ensured it. A final twist of the knife that had been lodged in his 
back for months. 
 
Rob’s mind raced back through the years, to where it all began. He remembered his childhood in 
the Bronx, the joy he found at Cornell, and the bitter sting of betrayal when his professor handed 
his thesis idea to another student. He remembered the struggles to maintain relationships with his
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
his thesis idea to another student. He remembered the struggles to maintain relationships with his 
bosses, the successes of his technologies, and the trusted partners who had stood by him—at least 
until the going got tough. 
 
Where were they now? Jim, Mike, Stacy... all of them had disappeared, jumping ship when the 
waters got rough, leaving Rob to weather the storm alone. This moment should have been a 
celebration, but instead, it felt bittersweet. 
 
And there was Terry, sitting quietly by his side, just as she always had. She was his anchor, his 
confidante. She had weathered the betrayals, the heartbreaks, and even when he had made the 
impossible decision to let her go... —but let's not make a spoiler here!
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
impossible decision to let her go... —but let's not make a spoiler here! 
 
Suddenly, the ballroom fell silent. The host’s voice echoed across the room: 
"Ladies and Gentlemen, it's time to award the winner of the Ernst & Young Silicon Valley 
Entrepreneur of the Year in Communications Technology Award. Mr. Rob Ryan." 
 
Rob was stunned. For a moment, he sat frozen, unsure if the recognition had finally come. Was this 
the acknowledgment he'd been waiting for, after all the battles and sacrifices? As he stood to make 
his way to the stage, a flood of emotions washed over him. This wasn’t just about the award; it was 
about the journey that had led him here. More than any other moment, it was the creation of the
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
about the journey that had led him here. More than any other moment, it was the creation of the 
Sunflower Code that had paved the way to this success. It had made him the king of his field. 
The Sunflower Code wasn’t just a tool—it was a revelation. And it wasn’t only Rob who benefited 
from it. Many entrepreneurs followed in his footsteps, using the same principles to achieve their 
own remarkable successes. 
 
This book will take you through that story. It will show you how Rob Ryan revolutionized not just 
the internet but the very way we think about entrepreneurship. The lessons from the Sunflower 
Code turned Ascend Communications into a $24 billion powerhouse, and they continue to inspire 
those who dare to dream big.
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
Code turned Ascend Communications into a $24 billion powerhouse, and they continue to inspire 
those who dare to dream big. 
 
With these seven powerful steps woven into the story, many have become —and will continue to 
become—the king of their industry, turning their passions into triumphs. This book might even be 
considered a new genre—a blend of drama and tutorial. After all, what better life stories are there 
than those that teach us a lesson? 
 
 
4 
 
The Meeting of Two Minds. 
A prologue 
 
The first time I met Rob Ryan was at a seminar he was giving on the "Sunflower Code" in Hawaii. I 
remember sitting there, listening to his talk —this brilliant entrepreneur with a deep capitalist
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
remember sitting there, listening to his talk —this brilliant entrepreneur with a deep capitalist 
mindset, speaking passionately about how his code had revolutionized the world of business. And 
as soon as the seminar ended, I walked up to him and said the words that would kickstart one of 
the most meaningful relationships of my life: "I don’t like people like you—capitalists with no sense 
of social responsibility." 
 
That moment, which could have ended in an argument, became the foundation of a deep friendship 
and an even deeper partnership. Rob, with his razor-sharp focus on entrepreneurship and economic 
success, and me, with my creative drive and passion to reshape human society through the power
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
success, and me, with my creative drive and passion to reshape human society through the power 
of media. It seemed like a mismatch at first, but the more we spoke, the more we realized that 
together, we had something extraordinary to offer the world. 
 
That chance encounter led to countless collaborations. First, we created "License2Dream", an 
educational program for students that combined our skills to teach the younger generation about 
entrepreneurship through 3D and interactive media. Then came The Ma n Who Saved the Internet 
with a Sunflower, a dramatic film that told the untold story of Rob's journey and how the Sunflower 
Code not only saved the internet but reshaped the landscape of technology and innovation.
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
Code not only saved the internet but reshaped the landscape of technology and innovation. 
 
And now, we bring you this book —a culmination of our shared journey, our experiences, and the 
countless lessons we’ve learned along the way. This book is not just about Rob’s "Sunflower Code"; 
it’s about how two very different people, from different worlds, joined forces to turn their ideas into 
reality. Together, we’ve set ambitious goals for the next decade, and we’re just getting started. 
Rob, as some of you may know, is investing heavily in anti -aging technology, so our plans include 
sticking around for quite a while! There’s a lot of work to do, and as the old saying goes, "Two are 
better than one." But in our case, we like to think "fou r are better than two" —because we can’t
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
better than one." But in our case, we like to think "fou r are better than two" —because we can’t 
forget our incredible partners, Terry and Irit, who have been with us every step of the way, making 
this journey richer and more meaningful. 
This is our story, and it’s far from over. 
 
Ori Yardeni

 Chapter 1: Seeds of Strategy 
 
“What do you think you’re doing, kid?” The man’s voice cut through the quiet of the golf course. 
Rob, always quick on his feet, had just plucked a ball from the rough and was turning it over in 
his hand—a pristine white golf ball, practically new. 
 
Except, it wasn’t his. 
 
He glanced up and saw the golfer storming toward him, his face twisted in anger. 
 
“That’s my ball! I’m about to hit my next shot!”
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
He glanced up and saw the golfer storming toward him, his face twisted in anger. 
 
“That’s my ball! I’m about to hit my next shot!” 
 
Rob wasn’t the type to flinch, even when confronted. He quickly weighed his options, his mind 
already calculating the strategies. 
 
“Didn’t seem like you were going to find it... so, I tried to help you,” he muttered under his 
breath. He tossed the ball back casually. “Here, take it. But if you lose it again, don’t worry... there 
are plenty of fresh balls in the pro shop.” 
 
The man muttered something as he walked away, shaking his head. Rob smirked to himself, his 
mind already racing to the next move. He knew how to push the envelope, but he also knew 
when to back off and live to fight another day.
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
mind already racing to the next move. He knew how to push the envelope, but he also knew 
when to back off and live to fight another day. 
 
Rob made his way back to the pro shop, where Mr. O’Reilly, the manager, stood behind the 
counter. 
 
“Mr. O’Reilly, got another batch for you,” Rob said, placing a handful of golf balls on the counter. 
 
“You’ve been busy, haven’t you, lad?” O’Reilly chuckled, counting the balls. “Just don’t ruffle too 
many feathers out there. Players don’t like losing their balls.” 
 
“Just trying to make an honest living,” Rob replied with a grin. “Besides, I’m only picking up the 
ones they’ve forgotten about.” 
 
O’Reilly shook his head but smiled. “You’re a smart one, Rob. Keep playing fair, and you’ll go far.”

ones they’ve forgotten about.”

But life wasn’t always fair, and Rob knew that well. His challenges that day weren’t over yet. On 
his way home, he rounded the corner and heard the familiar sound of taunting voices. Too late to 
change direction—they’d seen him. It was the neighborhood kids again, bored and looking for 
trouble. 
 
“Hey, brainiac!” one of them called out. “Still got your nose in those books?” 
 
Rob held his breath, his silence becoming a shield. 
 
“Hey Ryan, what’re you working on... inventing the wheel?” another sneered. 
 
“None of your business,” Rob replied without looking up. 
 
6 
 
“He’s a math freak... a new Einstein... lost in dreams,” one mocked.

text:
“None of your business,” Rob replied without looking up. 
 
6 
 
“He’s a math freak... a new Einstein... lost in dreams,” one mocked. 
 
Rob’s Irish temper flared. “It’s called thinking ahead. You should try it sometime.” 
 
The kid’s face twisted into a scowl. Rob knew where this was heading. They hated that he was 
different—that he thought faster, moved quicker. The boys closed in, but Rob didn’t wait for them 
to make the first move. 
 
"Strike first," he thought, recalling his father’s words. 
 
Without hesitation, Rob lunged at the biggest one, landing a solid punch. The kid doubled over in 
shock, and Rob bolted, running at full speed. The boys gave chase, but Rob was two steps ahead,

Chapter 14: The Internet Gamble 
 
The team scattered across the country, each tasked with a simple mission: figure out what was 
wrong with the internet and what it needed to survive. It didn’t matter if the client was running a 
major ISP or a small, dusty basement operation—everyone’s voice needed to be heard.
source: toIngest/the man who saved the internet with a sunflower -the story 20.9.pdf

text:
major ISP or a small, dusty basement operation—everyone’s voice needed to be heard. 
 
As the days passed, the answers were depressingly similar: frustration, disappointment, and 
mounting despair. ISPs were overcharging for a broken system that barely worked. The internet, 
once hailed as the future, was now teetering on the edge of collapse, with no one sure how to fix 
it. One evening, after another long day of meetings, Rob collapsed into his hotel bed, exhausted. 
Just as he closed his eyes, his phone buzzed on the nightstand. He glanced at the screen: it was 
Tim. 
 
“Hey, Tim,” Rob answered, his voice weary. 
 
“Rob, we’ve got a situation,” Tim began. “The board... well, it’s come to their attention that Terry’s 
no longer with Ascend.”

with a wink. "You've got a winning formula here."

"The internet," the student continued, "is responsible for so much pain now. Bullying, fake news, 
trolling—people can hurt each other without consequence. Does that make you proud?" 
 
The room fell silent, tension hanging in the air. 
 
Rob took a deep breath, feeling the weight of the question. "Look," he began slowly, "the internet 
is a tool, and like any tool, it can be used for good or for harm. But—" 
 
"Good?" the student interrupted, holding up a poster. It was a picture of a young boy, the words 
"How the Internet Killed Me" emblazoned beneath it. "That’s my younger brother. He took his own 
life after being bullied online." 
 
Rob’s heart sank. "I’m so sorry," he said quietly. "How old was he?"

But what did Rob mean exactly? Ori had learned by now that Rob’s emails were always cryptic

Fifteen minutes later, Ori and Rob met in the lobby. Rob looked around. “Where’s your wife, Irit?” 
 
“She’s in the gym,” Ori replied. 
 
“No, no, I need her here with you,” Rob laughed. “She’s the best translator you’ve got.” 
 
The two sat and talked for three hours. By the end of the conversation, they had shaken hands

initiative that would teach young people the principles of the Sunflower Code.

The Beginers Edition

2 
 
Introduction 
What sets this book apart other entrepreneurial guides are that, typically, business books are 
written by researchers and academics who analyze the successes and failures of others. 
Alternatively, successful entrepreneurs may share their personal biographies or hire ghostwriters 
to recount their stories. Rob Ryan, however, is doing something rare: he’s offering his own success 
formula. This is not just based on his personal achievements but on the wealth of experience he 
has gained mentoring countless entrepreneurs to extraordinary heights. In doing so, he has made 
it his life’s mission to help others grow and succeed, turning mentorship into his second great act 
of service to the world.

it his life’s mission to help others grow and succeed, turning mentorship into his second great act 
of service to the world. 
Rob Ryan's impact within the business world is monumental. As the mastermind behind Ascend 
Communications, the company that effectively saved the internet from collapse in the 1990s, Rob 
led his company to dominate the internet infrastructure market. The company was sold for an 
unprecedented $24 billion—at the time, the largest tech acquisition in history. This record stood 
for many years, cementing Rob's place as one of the most accomplished entrepreneurs of our 
time. The incredible story of Ascend's rise, and Rob’s role in transforming the digital landscape, is 
captured in the dramatic feature film "The Man Who Saved the Internet with a Sunflower" and

captured in the dramatic feature film "The Man Who Saved the Internet with a Sunflower" and 
the accompanying book. 
Since then, Rob has continued to achieve unparalleled success, with many of the companies he 
invested in and mentored going on to multi-billion-dollar sales. Entrepreneurs from across the 
globe have sought his guidance, eager to learn from his strategic insights and apply them to their 
own ventures. 
The "Sunflower Code", which played a pivotal role in Rob’s personal success, is at the heart of this 
guide. It is a logical, straightforward process that, when followed correctly, dramatically increases 
the chances of entrepreneurial success. In this book, we’ll break down the *Sunflower Code* into

the chances of entrepreneurial success. In this book, we’ll break down the *Sunflower Code* into

Introduction to the Sunflower Code 
The Sunflower Code is a strategic framework that has driven companies to multi-Billion-dollar 
successes. While it has been proven effective at large enterprises like Ascend Communications, it 
is equally powerful for individual entrepreneurs, small teams, and non-profit organizations. 
The Code serves as a crucial decision-making tool across various contexts, helping users cut 
through the noise of gut feelings with structured, critical thinking.

This approach is not just about making better business decisions; it’s about understanding the 
landscape of choices in any significant life decision. By dissecting decisions into their critical 
components, the Sunflower Code helps users ask the right questions and apply rigorous analysis, 
greatly improving the likelihood of success. Importantly, the ultimate aim is not only to establish a 
healthy and successful venture but also to dominate the field—to become the "King of" your 
sector.

4 
 
The 7 steps of the Sunflower in a nutshell 
 
 
"Step 1: Core Competencies" 
Identify your foundational strengths, which can be 
passions, skills, or resources. These will form the base 
from which you develop innovative products or services. 
 
 
"Step 2: UVL (Uniqueness, Value, Leverage)" 
Select competencies that are unique, offer significant 
value, and can be leveraged across various applications. 
Use a rating matrix to prioritize these key competencies. 
 
 
"Step 3: Petals" 
Transform your core competencies into potential products 
or services. Explore different scenarios and ideas that 
could lead to viable business opportunities. 
 
 
 
"Step 4: Matrix" 
Evaluate all potential products or services against criteria

could lead to viable business opportunities. 
 
 
 
"Step 4: Matrix" 
Evaluate all potential products or services against criteria 
like market demand and strategic fit. Use a scoring system 
to determine the most promising ideas. 
 
 
"Step 5: Walkabout" 
Validate your leading product or service idea by engaging 
with potential customers to gather real-world feedback 
and insights. 
 
 
 
"Step 6: Post-Walkabout" 
Analyze feedback from the Walkabout to decide whether 
to proceed with the current product, adjust it based on 
new insights, or pivot to a different idea. 
 
 
"Step 7: King of" 
Visualize your ultimate goal of market dominance. Create 
a strategic map that integrates your "King of" goal with key 
milestones and organizational functions.

5 
 
Step 1: 
Identifying Core Competencies 
Discovering Your Sunflower’s Center 
 
From Engineering to Haute Couture: A Natural Shift or a Missed Early Insight?" 
Bernard Arnault stands at the helm of LVMH, a global empire in the luxury goods industry, 
commanding revenues in the billions and defining haute couture worldwide. Yet, the origins of this 
colossal success story trace back to a markedly different beginning, heavily shaped by familial 
and societal expectations. Growing up in a family centered around engineering and project 
management, Arnault was steered toward attending École Polytechnique, France's leading 
engineering school, which later led him to a management position in his family's construction 
company.

engineering school, which later led him to a management position in his family's construction 
company. 
 
Was this the best fit for Arnault's deep-seated passions for art and creativity? It's unclear why, 
only after years in this role, Arnault experienced a pivotal eureka moment—realizing that his true 
calling lay not in concrete and cranes but in the craftsmanship and artistry of luxury goods. In 
1984, he acquired Financière Agache and then Christian Dior, marking his entry into the fashion 
industry and the beginning of the LVMH empire. Arnault's story underscores the importance of 
self-awareness and the courage to pursue a path that aligns with one’s personal strengths and 
passions. It highlights a common phenomenon where individuals may initially choose careers

passions. It highlights a common phenomenon where individuals may initially choose careers 
based on external influences, such as family legacy, without fully exploring their own interests and 
capabilities. 
This narrative mirrors the life stories of great innovators and entrepreneurs like Jeff Bezos, who 
started as a Wall Street analyst and emerged as a leader in e-commerce and other technological 
and entertainment empires. Other significant influencers who made substantial shifts in their 
careers after identifying their core competencies include Julian Assange, Ray Dalio, and Charles 
Phillips. Understanding this alignment is the first step of the Sunflower Code—recognizing your 
unique DNA and leveraging it to master the fields you are genuinely passionate about. This self-

inquiry aids in making pivotal decisions that are not merely reactive to societal expectations or 
immediate financial gain but are reflective of one's deepest values and capabilities. It teaches us 
that to truly excel and lead in any field, one must first understand and embrace their inherent 
strengths and passions. 
How you do it the right way? Right now! 
 
 
Ready to dive in? Let's jump straight into identifying your personal

Not Just an idea: Avoiding Empty Bubbles 
There is a tendency of entrepreneurs to wake up with new 
ideas and become emotionally attached to them as "blowing 
bubbles in the air." Many ideas fail not because the concept 
itself was flawed, but because the entrepreneurs did not have 
the core strengths required to execute it. The Sunflower Code 
emphasizes that while creativity is crucial, every idea must be 
evaluated rigorously through two key filters: internal 
capability and market feasibility. Entrepreneurs must ask 
themselves if they, or their team members, have all the core 
competencies needed to make the idea a reality. 
 
If they don't, can they realistically bring in the necessary expertise? Without this evaluation, even

If they don't, can they realistically bring in the necessary expertise? Without this evaluation, even 
the best ideas can fail. Alongside internal investigation, it's equally essential to validate the 
market demand for the idea. But we will deal with that later. Here are examples of failed 
innovations where the lack of core strengths of the team behind the idea played a significant 
role in the downfall: 
1. Theranos: Founded by Elizabeth Holmes, promised to revolutionize blood testing with a single 
drop of blood. While the concept was groundbreaking, there was no scientific basis to support 
such feasibility. The technical expertise to develop the device didn’t exist within the company’s 
team, and despite heavy investment and hype, Theranos failed spectacularly. Even after it

team, and despite heavy investment and hype, Theranos failed spectacularly. Even after it 
became clear that the technology wasn’t possible, Holmes continued to deceive investors, 
selling them a dream. The lack of both necessary scientific and engineering strengths, coupled 
with the failure of investors to demand solid proof, led to the company’s collapse. 
2. Pets.com: This dot-com era company aimed to capitalize on the growing internet marketplace 
by selling pet supplies online. While the concept wasn’t flawed, the company lacked 
expertise in logistics and e-commerce infrastructure to support the business at scale. 
Pets.com couldn’t handle the operational demands of the business, leading to its failure.

Pets.com couldn’t handle the operational demands of the business, leading to its failure. 
3. Webvan: Attempted to create an online grocery delivery service long before the current e-
commerce boom. The idea was sound, but the company lacked the operational strength to 
execute it. Without a strong logistics system or a deep understanding of supply chain 
management, Webvan quickly burned through capital and collapsed. 
These examples highlight the importance of following the Sunflower Code: entrepreneurs must 
first conduct a self-assessment of their core strengths and weaknesses. If the necessary skills or 
resources aren’t there, the idea is at risk, no matter how innovative it is. Before pursuing an idea,

resources aren’t there, the idea is at risk, no matter how innovative it is. Before pursuing an idea, 
it is crucial to build the right team and ensure you have the internal capabilities to execute it. 
Fostering Productive Teams: Diversity in Ideas, Strength in Competencies 
While it is essential to build a team where members complement each other’s core strengths, it is equally 
critical to ensure that there are no disruptive or unnecessary individuals in the team. These individuals can 
add strain and distract from the main objectives. The Sunflower Code values diversity in thought and 
encourages the exploration of revolutionary ideas. However, including team members who do not 
contribute to the core strengths or who are added for reasons such as personal connections or favoritism

contribute to the core strengths or who are added for reasons such as personal connections or favoritism 
(nepotism) must be avoided. Such additions often lead to inefficiency, hinder progress, and can even result 
in failure.

Welcome back! Now that you’ve identified your core competencies —skills, passions, and 
resources—it's time to refine these into tangible business or social enterprise ideas. This step will

Selecting Competencies: Begin by shortlisting competencies that meet the UVL criteria:

Selecting Competencies: Begin by shortlisting competencies that meet the UVL criteria: 
1. Uniqueness: Focus on what sets you apart. Each selected competency should offer a 
distinct advantage over others in your field. Innovation is a critical driver for standing out in 
a crowded market. 
2. Value: Ensure that each competency delivers real benefits to customers or the community. 
3. Leverage: Choose competencies that can be applied to create multiple products or 
services, maximizing their utility.

 Is Intuition a Reasonable Criterion in Our Matrix? 
Many people, particularly creative entrepreneurs, often 
rely on their gut feelings when making decisions. While 
intuition can sometimes guide us toward valuable insights, 
it can also lead us astray if not properly managed. The

intuition can sometimes guide us toward valuable insights, 
it can also lead us astray if not properly managed. The 
Sunflower Code emphasizes the importance of balancing 
intuition with structured analysis. 
Intuition should not be ignored, but it must be carefully 
evaluated and weighted based on the decision-maker's 
expertise. For individuals without significant experience in 
the field, intuition should play a minor role. In contrast, for 
those with deep knowledge, intuition can offer additional 
insights drawn from years of experience. 
 
Suggested Intuition Weighting: 
* Up to 5% for non-experts: When the decision-maker lacks experience in the area, intuition 
should account for no more than 5% of the decision, as it’s more likely to be swayed by cognitive

should account for no more than 5% of the decision, as it’s more likely to be swayed by cognitive 
biases or irrelevant factors. 
* Up to 15% for experts: For those with expertise, intuition can carry more weight—up to 15%. 
Experts often recognize patterns subconsciously, and their gut feelings are informed by their 
knowledge and past experiences. 
 
 
By keeping intuition within these limits, 
entrepreneurs can ensure it serves as a 
helpful guide without overwhelming the 
more critical factors of structured, data-
driven analysis.

Not Just an idea: Avoiding Empty Bubbles 
Entrepreneurs often fall in love with their ideas, becoming 
emotionally invested without fully considering whether 
those ideas are viable. Ideas that sound exciting but lack 
the foundation needed to succeed. The Sunflower Code 
emphasizes that while creativity is important, every idea 
must be rigorously tested against market feasibility. 
The first step is to conduct a thorough internal review —
does the entrepreneur or their team have the necessary 
skills, resources, and capabilities to execute the idea? If 
not, can those gaps realistically be filled? 
Once this internal assessment is complete, the next 
critical step is to validate the idea in the marketplace.

Once this internal assessment is complete, the next 
critical step is to validate the idea in the marketplace. 
Too often, excitement blinds entrepreneurs to whether the product has real demand and if 
consumers will be willing to pay a price that ensures success. 
Many innovative ideas have failed throughout history because they were not properly vetted: 
 LaserDiscs: A promising technology meant to replace VHS tapes, LaserDiscs failed to gain 
traction because consumers were still attached to VHS, and the cost was prohibitive. The product 
didn’t align with the market’s readiness, leading to its downfall. 
Segway: Once touted as the future of personal transportation, the Segway never became the

Segway: Once touted as the future of personal transportation, the Segway never became the 
mainstream hit it was predicted to be. While innovative, the developers overlooked key factors like 
practicality and affordability for everyday users. In a tragic twist, the founder of the company, Jimi 
Heselden, died after accidentally riding a Segway off a cliff—symbolically reflecting both the literal 
and figurative fall of the product.

Step 7: 
 
 
Introduction: What It Means to Be the "King of" Your Market 
Becoming the "King of" your market isn’t just about leading in revenue or being the largest player in 
your industry. It’s about strategically positioning your company to dominate a niche or sector by 
aligning your core competencies with your market’s biggest needs. This involves deep 
understanding of your strengths and leveraging them in a way that competitors can’t easily 
replicate. 
 
The key to "King of" success lies in focusing on unique strengths, anticipating market changes, 
and building sustainable advantages. Whether you’re in technology, retail, or any other field, the 
Sunflower Code provides a framework for achieving this market dominance. 
 
 
Defining Your "King of" Statement:

Sunflower Code provides a framework for achieving this market dominance. 
 
 
Defining Your "King of" Statement: 
What and where? 
 
The first step in any "King of" strategy is clarity. You 
must clearly define what it is you want to dominate 
and where. This could be a specific market, 
geographic region, or conceptual space such as 
innovation, sustainability, or user experience. 
 
Example: “We will be the King of eco-friendly office 
supplies in the Pacific Northwest by achieving 70% 
market share, securing partnerships with 90% of 
regional suppliers, and becoming known for the 
highest customer satisfaction. ”

your entire organization.

define dominance. 
2. Resource Control: Control over key resources that

 through feedback and repeat business.

The "Suffocation" Strategy 
Once your barriers to entry are in place, you need to implement a "suffocation" strategy—ensuring 
that competitors cannot catch up to your advancements. This involves setting detailed role-specific 
goals for each department, ensuring that every team member is aligned toward the company-wide 
"King of" objective. 
 
Creating Measurable Milestones

"King of" objective. 
 
Creating Measurable Milestones 
For each stratagem, set specific, measurable milestones that track progress toward your "King of" 
goal. Every goal should have clear timelines and metrics, ensuring that you can dynamically 
respond to new challenges and opportunities. 
Example: Ascend’s Strategy Implementation 
At Ascend Communications, Rob Ryan used the "King of" strategy to dominate the internet 
infrastructure market. By focusing on digital dialing as a core competency, Ascend was able to 
capture a significant portion of the market and continuously innovate in a way that kept competitors 
at bay. This was achieved by aligning every department’s goal with the overarching "King of" strategy.

32 
 
Innovation as the Core of Your Strategy 
No "King of" strategy can succeed without innovation. Companies that lead their industries are 
those that consistently innovate, staying ahead of the curve and setting the standards for others 
to follow. Innovation must be at the heart of your strategy, continually driving new products, 
services, and market opportunities. 
 
Conclusion: Adapting to Market Changes 
Becoming the "King of" your market isn’t a one-time achievement, it requires constant adaptation 
and vigilance. As market conditions change, competitors will try to find weaknesses in your 
strategy. By remaining focused on innovation, customer delight, and leveraging your core 
strengths, you can maintain your leadership position.

strategy. By remaining focused on innovation, customer delight, and leveraging your core 
strengths, you can maintain your leadership position. 
The "King of" strategy is a mindset. It’s about building a company that is not just the biggest, but 
the best, and whose competitors are always playing catch-up.

build and grow, adding new petals as market conditions evolve. Whether through technology, 
customer service, or product innovation, the Code provides a framework for becoming—and 
staying—the "King of" your market.

Key Insight: Solving a real, urgent problem that has significant costs or emotional pain to your

1. Customer Pain Points and Needs 
Key Insight: Solving a real, urgent problem that has significant costs or emotional pain to your 
customers can be the fastest way to market leadership. 
• High-tech Example: A SaaS (Software as a Service) company might focus on streamlining 
data privacy compliance for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) that are struggling to 
adapt to constantly evolving regulations like GDPR. By creating a simple tool that automatically 
updates compliance protocols, this company is addressing a critical pain point with large 
financial and legal implications for SMEs. 
• Low-tech Example: A local repair shop might find its "King of" advantage by offering a 
mobile repair service for appliances. The pain point? Customers don’t want the hassle of

transporting heavy appliances like washing machines to the shop. The repair shop that offers at-
home service without extra fees could become the "King of" its local market by solving a 
convenience problem no one else is addressing.

this restaurant could position itself as the "King of Convenience Meals" in the area.

"Crowdsourced Creativity: The Sunflower Code in Filmmaking" 
 
The film "The Man Who Saved the Internet with a Sunflower" is likely the first movie ever produced 
using the very invention it is about—an invention that saved the internet. Ori Yardeni the movie 
creator, applied the principles of the "Sunflower Code" throughout the production, making it one 
of the first films created through "crowdsourced wisdom", rather than just crowdfunding. The 
audience wasn’t merely involved financially; they played an active role in shaping the creative 
process itself, from the concept stage, through the script, and even up to the final decisions on 
casting and scenes. 
 
Using the Sunflower Code, Yardeni gathered feedback 
on various emotions—smiles, tears, excitement,

casting and scenes. 
 
Using the Sunflower Code, Yardeni gathered feedback 
on various emotions—smiles, tears, excitement, 
conflict, anger, satisfaction, and romance—from a 
diverse group of viewers. This data-driven approach 
allowed him to track and measure how well the film’s 
message resonated with the audience. More than 2 
million viewers had already provided feedback on 
Yardeni’s previous productions, and this time, focus 
groups helped shape major elements, including the 
title and even casting decisions. In fact, dozens of 
people watched audition tapes and provided input on 
who should play the lead roles. 
 
One particularly unique moment occurred during the filming of a scene where the lead actors,

who should play the lead roles. 
 
One particularly unique moment occurred during the filming of a scene where the lead actors, 
portraying Rob and Terry, were supposed to kiss. A disagreement arose between the director and 
the producer about whether it should be a cheek kiss or a French kiss. Since no consensus was 
reached, Yardeni suggested a live vote by the 100 extras and crew members on set. The crowd 
opted for a French kiss, and that’s how the scene was filmed and edited. 
 
The film’s production also coincided with the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic, when the 
world was forced to operate remotely. Much of the communication and coordination was done via 
video conferencing, a field Rob Ryan himself had championed, and large portions of the

video conferencing, a field Rob Ryan himself had championed, and large portions of the 
production were handled through the very internet that Ryan helped save.
